interviewStages: Behavioral: ownership, navigating ambiguity, the customer-trust framing
codingTopics: OO design: model a business domain such as workflows with triggers and actions
Multi-tenant data structures: per-tenant rate limiting, isolation
Streaming: rolling aggregates over tenant events
Graph and DAG: workflow evaluation, dependency resolution
Search and filters: AND/OR queries over records
systemDesignTopics: Design an alert routing system with escalation policies and overrides
Design a notification fanout: SMS, voice call, push, email
Design a status page that aggregates signal from multiple integrations

coding: At PagerDuty, model a workflow engine with triggers, conditions, and actions. Evaluate a workflow DAG and detect cycles.
Implement a per-tenant rate limiter so one customer cannot exhaust shared capacity. Discuss the token-bucket design.
Given a stream of tenant events, maintain rolling per-entity scores with bounded memory.
Implement a filter query over records supporting AND/OR on custom fields. Discuss indexing for a multi-tenant store.
Deduplicate records via fuzzy clustering on name and email. Discuss the matching and complexity.
behavioral: Tell me about a project you are proud of at PagerDuty scale and the hardest tradeoff you made.
Give me an example that shows ownership, navigating ambiguity, the customer-trust framing.
Describe a time you disagreed with a teammate or manager. How did it resolve?
Tell me about a time you took ownership of an ambiguous problem end to end.
systemDesign: Design an alert routing system with escalation policies and overrides.
Design a notification fanout: SMS, voice call, push, email.
Design a status page that aggregates signal from multiple integrations.
values: PagerDuty succeeds when its customers do. Describe a time you solved for the customer even at a cost to the team.
Tell me about a time you handled a tricky multi-tenant isolation or permissions problem.
How do you balance enterprise customization against product simplicity?
other: How does PagerDuty compare to Opsgenie?
What is the engineering culture like?

PagerDuty Interview Guide (2026): Incident Response Engineering
By 			
				
				techinterview.org			
			
		

		 /  May 5, 2026 
			
		

	
	
	

		
		
On this page

- Process

- What they actually ask

Updated May 5, 2026 · techinterview.org

⏱ 1 min read

PagerDuty
pagerduty.com ↗

PagerDuty is the canonical incident management platform — when your prod system breaks, PagerDuty is what wakes the on-call engineer. The interview emphasizes reliability engineering, 24/7 system design, and the human-systems aspects of handling alerts at scale.

Process

Recruiter screen → 60-minute coding phone (DSA medium) → onsite virtual: 2 coding, 1 system design, 1 craft deep-dive, 1 behavioral. Cycle: 3–4 weeks.

What they actually ask

- Design an alert routing system with escalation policies and overrides

- Design a notification fanout: SMS, voice call, push, email — at sub-second latency

- Design a status page that aggregates signal from multiple integrations

- Coding: medium DSA, often graph or tree problems

- Behavioral: ownership, navigating ambiguity, the customer-trust framing

Levels and comp (2026)

- SE II: $150K–$185K total

- Senior SE: $220K–$280K

- Staff: $300K–$400K

- Principal: $420K–$560K

Prep priorities

- Read about Tier-1 reliability practices: SRE workbook, Google SRE book

- Be fluent in JVM languages (Scala dominates the codebase) or willing to ramp up

- Understand telephony integrations and the realities of carrier delivery latency

Frequently Asked Questions

Is PagerDuty remote-friendly?

Hybrid in San Francisco, Toronto, Atlanta, and Lisbon. Many engineering roles are fully remote within the supported countries.

How does PagerDuty compare to Opsgenie?

PagerDuty is the dominant brand; Opsgenie (Atlassian) is the budget enterprise alternative. PagerDuty has stronger US enterprise penetration and richer API ecosystem.

What is the engineering culture like?

Reliability-obsessed. Senior engineers do real on-call. Outages are studied with rigor. Strong post-mortem and blame-free culture.
